The tool supports by-number-of-teams or max-team-size mode, optional bench leftovers, duplicate removal, generated team names, and copy result. The page claims local generation and no account requirement. A JavaScript console error about dedupeNames appeared during testing, and the core generator produced teams anyway.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required; the broader Spacebar Count site has account/pro navigation.
tools.overallComment: A polished simple splitter with useful bench/dedupe options, despite a console error.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names, choose split mode, and generate.
ratingComments.design: Readable modern cards with themed team names.
ratingComments.features: By team count/max size, bench leftovers, dedupe, and generated names are useful simple-tool features.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy result is available; no CSV/PDF/export was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: The page says generation is local, and only the site plus Google fonts were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for the generator, although account navigation exists.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams only, without constraints or repeat minimization.

pros: By team count or max team size
tools.reviewNote: Live test generated three teams. Console error observed: dedupeNames is not defined.

tools.name: TeamGenerator.net Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Sports and game teams balanced by skill ratings and roles
tools.summary: TeamGenerator.net is a sports/game-oriented team balancer rather than a plain paste-and-shuffle page. Live testing added twelve players with 1–5 star skill ratings and roles, generated three teams of four, and produced evenly balanced averages with role distribution across teams. It supports by-number-of-teams and by-players-per-team modes, role/position input, local saved lists, import/export for saved lists, Copy All, per-team copy, and Generate Again. The downside is that participants must be added one by one; there is no simple bulk paste roster workflow, no CSV/spreadsheet export, no repeat planning, and privacy is weakened by Google Analytics/AdSense/Funding Choices.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely useful skill/role balancer for sports and games, but slower to load rosters than paste-based tools and not privacy-friendly.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The UI is clear, but adding every participant one at a time with stars and optional role is slower than paste-based rosters. Saved-list import/export helps repeat use.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and focused, with good visual team cards and star/role display. Ads and consent UI add page noise.
ratingComments.features: Above-average for sports/game teams: skill ratings, roles/positions, by-team-count and by-players-per-team modes, local saved lists, import/export, Copy All, per-team copy, and regenerate. Missing bulk paste, arbitrary attributes, hard constraints, repeat planning, saved result history, and deeper diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy All and per-team copy are useful, and saved-list import/export helps with rosters. There is no CSV/spreadsheet result export, TXT/JSON download, image/PDF, Zoom export, share link, or report format.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The tool says data is saved only in the browser and no generation API upload was observed, but the page uses Google Analytics, Google AdSense/DoubleClick, Funding Choices, and a Google-managed consent dialog saying data may be shared with 210 partners and precise geolocation may be processed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, or payment is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than random-only splitters for sports and games because it balances player counts, skill averages, and roles. It is not a broad constraint solver or multi-round optimizer.

cons: No hard constraints or repeat avoidance
Sports/player model is narrower than general group planning

tools.name: AI225 Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Browser-side random teams with import, exclude toggles, history, copy, and export
tools.summary: AI225 Team Generator is a capable basic team splitter inside a large online-toolbox site. Live testing produced balanced teams and exposed import/sample/clear, by-count/by-size modes, quick exclude toggles, result history, copy, and export. The tool claims local calculation and no data upload. It is more complete than most plain randomizers, but it is not a constraint solver and the page loads Clarity, Baidu, Umami, and Cloudflare scripts.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A strong simple splitter with useful workflow controls, held back by tracking scripts and no advanced grouping logic.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste/import and generate worked cleanly in live testing.
ratingComments.design: Busy toolbox shell, but the generator panel is clear.
ratingComments.features: By-count/by-size modes, import, quick exclude, history, copy, and export are useful for a basic tool.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy and export controls were visible after generation; the page describes TXT export.
ratingComments.privacy: The tool claims local processing, but Microsoft Clarity, Baidu, Umami, and Cloudflare scripts loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random assignment only.

tools.reviewNote: Live test generated two balanced groups from twelve names; quick exclude toggles were visible.

tools.name: ClassInteractives Formgroup Creator
tools.bestFor: UK-style form/class allocation using friends and must-avoid relationships
tools.summary: ClassInteractives Formgroup Creator is a specialized school allocation tool rather than a generic random team generator. Users paste or upload a completed CSV/template, choose the number of forms or next-year classes, process the allocation, inspect close/wider friend counts, use color-coded relationship highlighting, swap students by right click, and download a results CSV. It targets the hard school transition problem of balancing forms while respecting friendships and must-avoid pairings. The UI is sparse and brittle, input formatting is easy to get wrong, and it lacks modern explanations, privacy policy context, or broad export options.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Niche but genuinely useful for form allocation; too brittle and specialized for general group generation.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The workflow is not beginner-friendly: users need the template and correct relationship columns, then wait for processing.
ratingComments.design: Very plain Bootstrap-style interface. Functional, not polished.
ratingComments.features: Specialized strengths: CSV/template workflow, close friends, wider friends, cannot-be-with rules, workload/gender-style columns, manual swap checks, forms/classes modes, and result download.
ratingComments.outputs: Download Results CSV is available after processing. No PDF/image/share link or rich report.
ratingComments.privacy: No account and very few third-party requests beyond jsDelivr in testing. Privacy documentation is minimal.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Potentially strong for its narrow school-form allocation problem because it accounts for friendships/avoid rules and manual swaps.

tools.reviewNote: Template header is Name, Gender, Best Friends, Wider Friends, Cannot be with, Higher workload. Input parsing is easy to misuse.

tools.name: CodersTool Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Reusable random groups with seed, history, sharing, TXT/CSV, and saved lists
tools.summary: CodersTool Random Group Generator is a strong random-group workbench rather than a solver. Paste names, choose number-of-teams or members-per-team mode, optionally set organizers per group, use a seed, save/load lists, generate, reroll, lock or reroll individual groups, copy groups, export TXT/CSV, and keep recent runs. The output is clear and balanced for basic random splitting. It does not provide real keep-apart/keep-together rules, attributes, repeat optimization, or a pair matrix, and the page has a heavy ad/analytics footprint.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: One of the better plain-random tools because of seed, history, saved lists, locks, share, and TXT/CSV outputs.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The main flow is understandable, though the page has many controls and ads around the workbench.
ratingComments.design: The workbench is more polished than most SEO-tool randomizers, with useful counters, preview text, run history, and per-group actions.
ratingComments.features: Good randomizer features: two split modes, organizer/representative selection, seed, saved lists, recent runs, share link, group lock/reroll, and sample datasets. No real constraint or attribute model.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy groups, TXT export, CSV export, share link, per-group copy, and run-history copy are all useful.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor for rosters: Google ads, Tag Manager/Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, Cloudflare/CDN scripts, and ad-quality domains loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random groups with reproducibility and reroll controls. No optimization beyond random splitting.

pros: By team count or members per team
tools.reviewNote: The page label says optional constraints, but live-visible controls did not establish a full constraint engine.

tools.name: DateRounds
tools.bestFor: Small free speed-dating or networking rotation events
tools.summary: DateRounds is the dating-specific sibling to FastRounds and exposes a working no-signup host flow. Live testing created a free host event after a simple captcha and reached a host dashboard with a code, opposite-gender matching, same-gender matching, any-gender networking mode, maximum rounds, round timing, age-range preference, strict age filtering, mutual-match messaging, and manual/automatic starts. It is a real free rotation generator for small events, but the six-attendee cap and dating-specific assumptions make it narrower than a general group generator.
tools.pricing: Free host event with no signup or credit card for up to six attendees. Larger events require paid event packs or subscriptions.
tools.overallComment: A useful small-event speed-dating/networking rotation host, with better matching controls than FastRounds but the same strict free cap.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The free host flow works, but the configuration screen has many dating-specific options that can overwhelm basic networking use cases.
ratingComments.design: Plain but practical host dashboard. It feels operational rather than polished.
ratingComments.features: Opposite-gender, same-gender, any-gender networking, age preferences, strict age matching, mutual-match messaging, timing, round counts, and start modes are substantial for this niche.
ratingComments.outputs: Like FastRounds, it is built for live running rather than exporting a reusable static schedule.
ratingComments.privacy: Hosted attendee/event flow with matching and optional messaging; not local/private.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The tested free event required no signup or credit card.
ratingComments.resultQuality: For small speed-dating/networking events, the matching controls are meaningful. It does not provide broad optimization diagnostics.

pros: No signup or credit card for a capped free event
cons: Free version capped at six attendees
tools.reviewNote: Live test waited out the anti-spam throttle, created a no-signup host event, and observed host code 76372 plus opposite-gender, same-gender, networking, age-range, strict-filter, mutual-message, timing, and start-mode controls.

tools.name: Random-Group-Generator.com
tools.bestFor: Single-session browser grouping with copy/export/share controls and lightweight balance modes
tools.summary: Random-Group-Generator.com is much more capable than a bare random splitter, but the marketing oversells it. The actual tool supports pasted names, file upload, normal/level/gender modes, group-count control, local-processing messaging, regenerate, auto-balance, copy result, export, share, drag-and-drop refinement, login-gated saving, and a simple group-analysis score. That is a decent one-session workflow. It is not, however, a serious multi-session repeat-minimization solver, and the page has Google Ads, Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, and a Funding Choices consent stack.
tools.pricing: Free for basic generation. Saving/history uses login. The page advertises optional sync/account features rather than a clearly priced paid plan.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely useful one-session group generator with good outputs and some balancing controls, held back by ad-tech/privacy issues and marketing claims that go beyond the visible product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic flow is straightforward: paste names, set group count, generate. Extra mode/export/save controls are visible without making the quick path too hard.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and more product-like than most randomizer pages. The long marketing page and ad/consent layer make it feel less clean.
ratingComments.features: Good for one session: file upload, normal/level/gender modes, regenerate, auto-balance, drag-and-drop refinement, analysis score, export, share, and login-gated history. No verified multi-round repeat optimization or serious constraint solver.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong output surface: copy result, export, share, and login-gated saving/history are available from the result panel. Not a verified Zoom-specific CSV workflow despite the marketing copy.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed to bad. The tool says names are processed locally and the privacy policy says saved/account data is handled for sync, but the page also loads Google Ads, Funding Choices, Google Tag Manager, and Microsoft Clarity.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Basic generation works without login. Saving/history and some reuse workflows are account-gated.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than plain random splitting because balance modes, auto-balance, drag-and-drop refinement, and analysis exist. Not comparable to a repeat-optimization or hard-constraint solver.

cons: Some marketing claims exceed the visible tool
Google Ads, Funding Choices, GTM, and Microsoft Clarity
No clearly documented pricing for account features

tools.name: Sport Scheduler Pro Free Schedule Generator

It deserves inclusion because it freely generates group/team schedules, but it is a league scheduler, not a general participant group optimizer.

ratingComments.features: Team count, custom names, weeks, dates/times, public/admin links, rosters, scores, standings, venues, and schedule tabs are useful. It lacks general grouping constraints.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV, PDF, copyable admin/public links, public schedule pages, and week-by-week schedule views are strong for a free generator.

tools.name: FastRounds

tools.summary: FastRounds provides a working no-signup host flow for speed-networking and team-building rotations. tools.name: ChatGPT (Free Version)
tools.bestFor: Natural-language grouping when users are willing to verify the output
tools.summary: ChatGPT Free can create group assignments from a prompt, including arbitrary team counts, rough balancing, keep-together/keep-apart requests, and CSV-like output. tools.pricing: Free tier available with usage/model/tool limits. Higher limits, stronger models, and business privacy defaults require paid ChatGPT plans; free consumer chats may be used to improve models depending on data controls/settings.
tools.overallComment: Flexible enough to be useful, but risky as a group generator because correctness is not guaranteed. Good for drafts, not trustworthy without manual verification.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy if the request is simple and the user knows how to prompt. Harder than a purpose-built splitter because you need to describe rules clearly and then audit the answer for missing names, duplicates, or violated constraints.

ratingComments.features: High flexibility, but not productized group-generation depth. It can attempt arbitrary team counts, group sizes, balancing, keep-together/keep-apart rules, multi-round schedules, comments, and custom output formats. The catch is that these are language-model behaviors, not validated solver features; free-tier limits/context can also get in the way.
ratingComments.outputs: Can produce tables, Markdown, CSV-like text, explanations, and revised versions on request. There is no native group roster export button, Zoom export, persistent scenario file, or structured diagnostics unless the user asks and verifies the text.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor fit for sensitive rosters unless configured carefully. Free/consumer ChatGPT may use submitted content to improve models depending on settings; chats are stored unless deleted or temporary chat is used; and users should not paste student/personnel data without the right account, consent, and data controls.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Free access exists and some use is possible without signing in, but practical saved workflows, higher limits, file/context features, and data controls depend on account state and plan limits.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Can be surprisingly good for small, clearly specified grouping tasks, but it can also make silent mistakes. No deterministic seed, no guaranteed constraint satisfaction, no repeat matrix, and no solver validation. Manual checking is mandatory.

cons: Must manually check duplicates, omissions, and rule violations
Poor default fit for sensitive rosters

ratingComments.resultQuality: Fine random teams, with custom labels. It does not optimize membership quality or manage repeat pairings.

tools.name: Diverse Group Generation
tools.bestFor: Small attribute-balanced grouping experiments
tools.summary: Diverse Group Generation is a prototype-style balanced grouping tool. Instead of pasting names, you build a member table made of attributes and options, then choose the number of groups. It runs an optimization/genetic-algorithm approach to make each group’s attribute distribution resemble the overall member distribution. That is much more interesting than a plain random splitter, but the workflow is narrow: members are IDs, setup is manual, there is no roster import, no names-first workflow, no constraints, no export, and no saved scenarios.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely interesting balancing prototype with better assignment logic than most simple randomizers, but too rough and output-limited for normal classroom or workshop use.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The concept is clear once understood, but the names-first workflow most people expect is missing. You have to build attributes/options and edit member rows manually.
ratingComments.design: Clean enough for a prototype, with collapsible sections and visible distribution counts. Some UI text and duplicate-looking options are rough.
ratingComments.features: The important feature is real attribute-distribution balancing across multiple attributes. It lacks many practical features: import/export, named rosters, constraints, repeat handling, saved scenarios, and result editing.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are shown as tables of member IDs and attribute distributions. There is no obvious CSV, copy, PDF/image, share link, or structured export.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is required and the app appears lightweight, but the page loads Google advertising infrastructure and Vercel analytics. There is no strong privacy posture for roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: For attribute-balanced grouping, the assignment logic is more serious than random splitting. It only optimizes the simple attribute table it is given and offers no constraints or repeat history.

cons: Members are displayed as IDs rather than normal roster names
Prototype-like UI and limited practical workflow
tools.reviewNote: Score as a balancing prototype, not as a polished classroom roster tool. Its result quality deserves credit for attribute optimization, while usability/output should stay low until it supports names, import/export, and practical review/edit workflows.

tools.pricing: Free. No account is required for the generator.
tools.overallComment: A decent basic team generator with share/export and skill-balance claims, slowed by manual input and rough implementation.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding players one by one works, but is slower than paste-first roster tools. Import exists but was not deeply tested.
ratingComments.design: Result cards are readable and the page explains the workflow, but the huge tool navigation around it is noisy.
ratingComments.features: Player list, import, team count, custom team names, skill-balance option, share/export, and generated team cards are useful. No constraints, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Share and Export buttons appear after generation. Exact export format was not deeply verified.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is needed, but Google Tag Manager and Google ad/quality domains loaded. React errors appeared in testing.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup is needed for generation, though a Sign In button exists.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random groups with optional skill-balancing claim. No transparent scoring or solver report.

tools.name: WheelPlay Team Picker Wheel
tools.bestFor: Visual team picking with CSV export, representatives, and label/gender distribution options
tools.summary: WheelPlay Team Picker Wheel is a feature-rich but confusing visual team picker. Live testing eventually worked after adding names, setting pick quantity to all participants, setting three groups, and starting the picker; it generated three groups of four and offered a Groups Board plus CSV download. The page also advertises gender/label distribution, representative picking, custom team names, and preset same/different group rules. The downside is serious UX friction: if pick quantity is left wrong, it can generate only one selected person, and the page loaded unusual third-party hosts.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Potentially powerful for a free visual picker, but easy to misconfigure and not privacy-clean.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The live test required discovering that pick quantity must be set to all participants before normal grouping works.
ratingComments.design: Visual wheel/group-board interface is useful, but the control model is cluttered.
ratingComments.features: Gender/label distribution, representative picking, CSV download, custom team names, and preset same/different group options are stronger than a plain splitter.
ratingComments.outputs: Groups Board and Download Result (.csv) were visible after successful generation.
ratingComments.privacy: No login is needed, but CloudFront, Google fonts, a QuikNode endpoint, and suspicious .beer script hosts loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Can create balanced random groups; advanced balancing/rules were not deeply validated.

cons: Easy to generate the wrong result
tools.reviewNote: Live test worked only after explicitly setting pickQuantity to 12. Initial generic test selected one participant into one group.

tools.name: Classroom.Group Online Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want a simple local web splitter and may later upgrade to the iOS Groups app
tools.summary: Classroom.Group Online Group Generator is the web companion to the Groups iOS app. The online tool lets you add student names, choose grouping by size or count, generate groups, and view previous groups. It says groups are generated locally and names are saved locally on the device. The more interesting features — avoided peers, gender balancing, unlimited classes, bulk import, PDF/print/export, and iCloud sync — are in the native app, not the web generator.
tools.pricing: Online generator is free. The iOS app is free with in-app purchases: Pro Monthly $2.99, Pro Yearly $14.99, or Pro $39.99.
tools.overallComment: A clean teacher-focused web splitter with good local-data posture, but most serious features live in the paid/native app.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The web flow is understandable, but adding students one by one is slower than pasting a whole roster into a textarea.
ratingComments.design: Clean, teacher-friendly, and uncluttered. Much better than generic ad-heavy randomizer pages.
ratingComments.features: The web tool has group size/count and previous groups. Avoided peers, gender balancing, classes, bulk import, export/print, and sync are app features, not web features.
ratingComments.outputs: Weak on the web: visible results and previous groups, but no clear copy, CSV, PDF, print, or share workflow. The native app has export and print.
ratingComments.privacy: Good for the web tool because generation and names are described as local. The site uses Google Analytics, and the app privacy policy lists Firebase/Crashlytics/TelemetryDeck usage and diagnostics.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The web generator does not require an account. Advanced app features require installing the app and some features are Pro/in-app purchase.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above a bare splitter because it avoids impossible/singleton group outputs and keeps previous groups, but the web tool is random-only.

pros: Native app offers avoided peers, gender balancing, import, export, and print
cons: Most advanced features require the app
tools.reviewNote: Scope the score to the online generator while mentioning the separate native Groups app where relevant.

tools.name: DecideHub Team Generator

tools.summary: DecideHub Team Generator is a clean sports-oriented random team tool. Live testing pasted twelve names and generated two balanced teams of six. It supports quick team-count buttons from two to six, regenerate, copy results, sharing, and locally saved player lists. It is easy and lightweight, but it has no group-size mode, constraints, attributes, repeat avoidance, export files, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A pleasant basic team generator with local roster saving, limited to simple random teams.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names and choose a team-count button. The workflow is straightforward.
ratingComments.design: Clean sports-themed page with readable result cards.
ratingComments.features: Regenerate, copy results, share, and local saved rosters are useful. Advanced grouping features are absent.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy Results and Share controls were visible; no file export was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Only decidehub.app was observed in the broad test.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams only.

tools.name: FindUtils Random Picker / Team Mode
tools.bestFor: Privacy-leaning browser-side team splits inside a multipurpose random picker
tools.summary: FindUtils Random Picker includes a working Team Generator mode. tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A useful privacy-leaning basic team mode, limited to simple balanced random groups.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Team mode works, but users must select the correct tab from a multipurpose random-picker UI.
ratingComments.design: Modern page with clear form/results, but much surrounding content.
ratingComments.features: Team-count splitting and copy are the main relevant features.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy control appeared after generation; no file export was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: The page claims client-side processing, but Clarity, Google Analytics, and Tag Manager loaded.

ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams only.

tools.name: Microsoft Excel
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet users who want local files or organization-controlled rosters
tools.summary: Microsoft Excel can be used as a DIY random group generator with RAND/RANDBETWEEN formulas, random sorting, templates, or macros. It is powerful for rosters, exports, and offline/organization-controlled workflows, but like Google Sheets it is not a purpose-built group generator unless the user builds or downloads a template.
tools.pricing: Included in Microsoft 365 plans or standalone Office licenses; Excel for the web is available with a Microsoft account, and organization/education pricing varies.
tools.overallComment: Slightly better than Google Sheets for privacy/offline control, but a DIY spreadsheet workflow rather than a real group-generator product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not easy as a group generator. Excel users can build a random grouping sheet with formulas or templates, but this is setup work, not paste names and click generate.
ratingComments.design: Excellent spreadsheet UI for people who know Excel, but no purpose-built grouping interface unless you build one with a template, macro, or workbook design.
ratingComments.features: Flexible but DIY. Excel can handle arbitrary team counts, group sizes, saved rosters, notes, formulas, filters, imports, exports, macros, Power Query, and custom templates. Constraints, balancing, and repeat handling are only partial because users must design the logic themselves.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent output surface: copy/paste, XLSX, CSV, PDF/print, local files, OneDrive/SharePoint sharing, versioning, and organization workflows. Stronger than most dedicated simple randomizers on export alone.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than many web tools because workbooks can stay local/offline or inside an organization-controlled Microsoft 365 tenant. Not automatically private: cloud storage, connected experiences, diagnostic/service data, links, macros, and sharing permissions all need care.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Desktop Excel requires a license or Microsoft 365 access, and Excel for the web requires a Microsoft account. Organizations may already have it, but it is not a no-account web tool.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic formula-based random groups are normal random-splitter quality. Better logic is possible with formulas/macros, but Excel has no built-in group solver, repeat minimizer, validation, or diagnostics.

pros: Can work offline or in controlled organization storage
Good for saved rosters, notes, and versioned workbooks
Extensible with formulas, templates, macros, and Power Query
cons: Microsoft account or license required for normal use
No built-in constraints or repeat optimizer
tools.reviewNote: Review as Excel used directly for grouping, not a specific third-party Excel template. Credit offline/local and organization-controlled workflows, but keep grouping scores below purpose-built generators because the logic is user-built.

tools.name: QuickWheelSpin Team Picker
tools.bestFor: Visual basic team picking with copy/share-image outputs
tools.summary: QuickWheelSpin Team Picker is a simple wheel-site team generator. The UI supports entry add/edit/remove/clear, entry presets, number-of-teams mode, a split-method control that advertises split-by-team or players-per-team behavior, dark mode, language controls, and local browser storage for entries. The tested share-link creation fell back to an error URL because the Supabase share endpoint failed DNS resolution, so share links are only partially credited. It has no attributes, constraints, skill/gender balancing, repeat minimization, multi-round planning, file/spreadsheet export, saved scenarios, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A polished basic random team picker with good copy/social-image sharing, but no real balancing logic and a share-link failure in live testing.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The built-in sample names make the tool immediately testable, and generate/copy are straightforward. Bulk paste is claimed in the page copy, but the visible tested workflow centered on entry rows and add/edit/remove controls rather than a clear paste textarea.
ratingComments.design: Modern and pleasant, with a focused app panel, modal results, dark-mode/language controls, and clear entry management. Some icon-only controls require exploration.
ratingComments.features: Basic team generation with entry add/edit/remove/clear, presets, number-of-teams controls, a split-method control for team count versus players-per-team behavior, dark mode, and local stored entries. No file import/export, attributes, constraints, saved scenarios, repeat planning, or advanced balancing.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than many basic splitters: Copy Teams, Share Results, social share buttons, and image downloads for share/Instagram story are visible. Live share-link creation produced an error-fallback URL because the Supabase endpoint failed, and there is no CSV, Excel, PDF, or Zoom export.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The privacy policy says wheel lists/settings are stored in browser local storage and shared-result data is temporarily stored on servers for about five minutes. The site uses Google Analytics, cookies/tracking language, and a Supabase endpoint for share links.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, payment, or verification was needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random team sizes, but no skill balancing, gender/attribute balancing, constraints, repeat avoidance, or quality diagnostics.

Number-of-teams and advertised players-per-team split modes

tools.name: Spinwheely Random Team Generator

tools.overallComment: A good-looking simple random team splitter with unusually strong exports, but no bulk paste and no real assignment intelligence.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The UI is clear once loaded, but adding members one by one is slower than paste-based team generators. The first load also showed a transient React hydration error before the actual tool appeared.
ratingComments.design: Polished modern UI with clear setup/results tabs and visual team cards. It feels more like an app than an old utility page, though ads/account chrome add some clutter.
ratingComments.features: Decent simple-tool features: team count, naming style, max-members/equal-size setting, one-by-one member entry, results tab, clear controls, and exports. No bulk paste, imports, attributes, constraints, saved rosters, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong for a simple randomizer: export menu includes PDF, CSV, and text. No Zoom-specific export, shareable result links, spreadsheet import, or richer report/history workflow.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No login is needed for the random team generator itself, although login/signup buttons are present for broader Spinwheely features.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain balanced random teams. Useful for one-off splitting, but no constraints, attribute balancing, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

ratingComments.accountFriction: The simple card workflow can be used without an account.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random card assignment can mix people during activities, but it does not optimize assignments or reduce repeats algorithmically.

tools.name: Zoom breakout room assignment
tools.bestFor: Assigning live meeting participants directly into Zoom rooms
tools.summary: Zoom breakout rooms are not a general group generator, but they are a real in-meeting grouping workflow. Hosts can create up to 100 rooms, auto-assign participants evenly, assign manually, let participants choose rooms, pre-assign rooms with a CSV, save assignments for recurring meetings, and move people during the session. It is convenient inside Zoom and weak everywhere else.
tools.pricing: Breakout rooms are available in Zoom meetings, including free/basic contexts, but meeting duration, participant capacity, admin controls, and large-meeting needs depend on Zoom plan and account settings.
tools.overallComment: Very useful if the only destination is a live Zoom meeting. Not a general-purpose group generator and not a quality optimizer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy enough for hosts already comfortable with Zoom, but awkward as a group generator: breakout settings must be enabled, pre-assignment lives in the web portal, CSV setup uses email addresses, and participants often need to be signed in correctly.
ratingComments.design: The in-meeting breakout UI is functional and integrated into the meeting experience. It is not a dedicated planning interface, but it is much better than exporting from a randomizer and then manually recreating rooms.
ratingComments.features: Moderate for Zoom-specific grouping: automatic even assignment, manual assignment, participant self-select, room renaming, moving/swapping, up to 100 rooms, CSV pre-assignment, saved assignments for recurring meetings, timers, broadcast, and activity status. Missing general group-generator features: constraints, balancing attributes, repeat optimization, multi-session planning outside recurring-room saves, and diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The output is directly actionable inside Zoom, which is valuable. Outside Zoom it is weak: pre-assignment CSV import exists, but there is no normal roster export, spreadsheet/report output, or shareable assignment page for other tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Zoom has serious enterprise/security documentation, DPAs, and education compliance materials, but this is a hosted meeting platform processing participant identities, room names, telemetry/service data, chats/recordings depending on settings, and account/admin data. Better than ad-tech utilities, not private like a local tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High friction for a “group generator”: the host needs a Zoom account/meeting, breakout settings enabled, and pre-assigned participants need matching Zoom accounts/emails for assignments to apply reliably.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Automatic assignment creates evenly sized rooms, which is fine for live one-off breakouts. It does not balance attributes, satisfy constraints, reduce repeats across varied sessions, or explain quality.

pros: Automatic, manual, and self-select room assignment
Can save breakout assignments for recurring meetings
Timers, broadcast, and activity status help live facilitation

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not easy as a group generator. Entering a roster is easy, but random grouping requires formulas, sorting, a copied template, or Apps Script. Spreadsheet-comfortable users will be fine; everyone else gets setup work.

ratingComments.features: Flexible but DIY. Sheets can handle arbitrary team counts, group sizes, saved rosters, notes, formulas, filters, imports, exports, and custom scripts. ratingComments.outputs: Excellent export and sharing surface: copy/paste, CSV, XLSX, PDF/print, share links, collaboration, version history, and integration with the broader Google ecosystem. Output is where Sheets is much stronger than most simple web randomizers.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. There is no ad-tech utility-page mess inside Sheets, and Workspace for Education has serious privacy terms. Rosters live in Google cloud under an account, sharing permissions can leak data if misconfigured, and consumer Google account data handling is not the same as a local/browser-only tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: A Google account is required for the normal workflow, and organizational users may need Workspace permissions. Not a no-account web splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic formula-based random groups are roughly normal random-splitter quality. Better results are possible with custom formulas/scripts, but there is no built-in solver, repeat minimization, constraint validation, or diagnostics.

ratingComments.outputs: Very weak for export. The visible product positioning is in-app classroom display/use, not CSV, Excel, Zoom breakout export, share links, PDFs, or reusable result reports.
ratingComments.privacy: ClassDojo has a much more serious student-privacy posture than ad-heavy utility sites: COPPA/FERPA/GDPR language, iKeepSafe certifications, Student Privacy Pledge, no selling/renting, and no student behaviorally targeted advertising. It is a hosted classroom platform collecting rosters, account data, device/usage data, and service-provider analytics, so it is not comparable to local/no-account tools.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High. The Group Maker is part of the ClassDojo app, not a no-login webpage. You need the ClassDojo ecosystem and likely a teacher account/class roster for real use.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above a plain random splitter because keep-apart sets can prevent bad pairings. It does not optimize balance, preferences, repeated encounters, or multi-session coverage.

Strong education privacy posture compared with ad-heavy utilities
cons: Mostly useful only for existing ClassDojo classrooms

It is useful if you are already on the article, but weak as a standalone group-generation product.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A working embedded classroom splitter buried in a cluttered, ad-heavy article page.

ratingComments.privacy: Raptive/AdThrive, consent manager, Clarity, Google analytics/tag manager, Slickstream, and related hosts were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random balanced groups only.

tools.name: MiniWebTool Random Group Generator

tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool with an anti-adblock/premium upsell popup. Premium is positioned as ad-free and faster; the related developer API starts free with credits and paid tiers.
tools.overallComment: A decent simple splitter, but the ad-supported/anti-adblock page environment drags it down. It is only in the basic-random-generator tier because it has no real grouping rules.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste one name per line, choose group size or number of groups, generate, copy. This deserves the same easy-workflow score as other obvious basic splitters.
ratingComments.design: The generated group cards are readable, but the page is a generic ad-supported utility page and the anti-adblock premium popup is intrusive. Not terrible, but clearly worse than the clean basic tools.
ratingComments.features: Basic group-generation feature depth. It supports arbitrary members-per-group or number-of-groups modes, balanced size distribution, local saved input, copy buttons, and an API. That is better than preset-only tools, but only earns about a 2 because there are no constraints, attributes, custom per-group capacities, multi-session planning, repeat optimization, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy buttons are useful, but this is a basic output surface. The consumer page does not provide CSV, Excel, Zoom export, share links, printable/PDF output, or persistent result history.
ratingComments.privacy: The tool page says names are stored only in browser local storage, which is good for roster privacy. ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for the web tool, which is good. The anti-adblock popup adds real access friction by asking users to allowlist the site or upgrade to Premium before continuing.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard one-off random grouping with even-ish group sizes. Consistent with the other plain random splitters: useful for a quick split, but no balancing, constraints, repeat reduction, or quality diagnostics.

cons: No CSV/Excel/Zoom export on consumer page

ratingComments.features: Basic splitter feature depth: paste names, choose number of groups or members per group, preview counts, and regenerate. No constraints, balancing attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics beyond counts.

tools.name: QuizBreaker Team Generator

Live testing found an important mismatch: the instructions say to put each name on a new line, but newline-separated names were treated as one member; space-separated names did generate three balanced teams from twelve sample names. It has a number-of-teams input and a Copy button, but no visible team-size mode despite the page copy, no custom team names, no exports beyond copy, no constraints, no attributes, no saved rosters, no repeat handling, and no diagnostics.

tools.overallComment: A weak random splitter. It technically works with space-separated names, but the documented newline workflow failed in live testing and the feature set is very shallow.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The intended UI is simple, but the documented one-name-per-line workflow failed in live testing because the script splits on spaces. Users can work around it with space-separated names, which is awkward for real rosters.
ratingComments.design: Modern and readable, but the actual generator is small and surrounded by a long QuizBreaker signup/product marketing page.
ratingComments.features: Very shallow: number-of-teams input, member textarea, random generation, and copy. No working newline roster paste, no team-size mode visible, no custom names, no constraints, attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeat planning, or diagnostics.

ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random teams, and the newline input bug can collapse a roster into one item. There are no constraints, balancing dimensions, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

It says it can handle up to 10,000 names, but it also sorts names alphabetically inside teams after assignment, which makes the result look less like a shuffled roster. There are no export controls, no group-size mode, and no real group-planning features.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Usable for a quick split, but very basic and output-poor. It belongs near the bottom of the simple random splitter tier.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough: paste names, enter number of teams, generate. The surrounding calculator-site navigation is more clutter than the tool needs.
ratingComments.design: Old-fashioned calculator-page design. Functional, but not especially pleasant or focused.
ratingComments.features: Just number of teams and a textarea. No group-size mode, constraints, attributes, leaders, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: On-page results only. No copy button, CSV, spreadsheet export, print view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required, but the page loads Google Tag Manager/Analytics and other third-party scripts. There is no strong roster-specific privacy posture on the tool page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment, then names are sorted alphabetically inside each team. That is fine for a trivial split, but it adds no assignment-quality value.

pros: Can handle large pasted lists according to the page
tools.reviewNote: This is a small calculator-site utility, not a dedicated group assignment product.

tools.name: GoSpinWheel Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Live wheel-based team assignment when presentation matters more than balanced rosters
tools.summary: GoSpinWheel Random Group Generator is a polished wheel page for assigning one person at a time to a random team. It is fun for a live classroom or game-night reveal, with editable team labels, weights, colors, result history, and share-card output. It is not a real roster-to-groups generator: the advertised workflow is to spin for each person, so balanced sizes, constraints, repeat avoidance, imports, and proper group-roster outputs are all missing or manual workarounds.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web wheel. Core spinning and editing work without an account; sign-in or paid plans are tied to public share links, cloud library, and ad-free use.
tools.overallComment: A good-looking spinner, but a weak group generator. It is better understood as a presentation-oriented random team assignment workaround than as a tool that generates balanced groups.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Editing team labels and spinning the wheel is straightforward. As a group generator, the workflow is clunky because participants are not pasted into a roster and split at once; the user has to call each person and spin repeatedly, or build a multi-wheel workaround.
ratingComments.design: Modern, colorful, and engaging for live presentation. The wheel UI, sounds, themes, and result modal look much better than most old utility pages, but ads, consent UI, sign-in prompts, and generic wheel-site controls add clutter.
ratingComments.features: Strong spinner features do not translate into strong group-generation features. It has editable entries, weights, colors, visual themes, result history, local saves, share-card output, and general wheel import/export, but no real team-size controls, constraints, roster import, attribute balancing, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Useful for sharing a single spin result: copy/download a winner image, social share buttons, and a results tab. Weak for actual groups because it does not produce a complete team roster, CSV/spreadsheet export, Zoom breakout output, or printable group assignment report.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor for sensitive rosters. The page uses a Google-managed consent/ad stack, AdSense/Funding Choices, Google Analytics, and ads for anonymous/free users. The privacy policy says anonymous visitors can save locally, but public share links and cloud library entries store wheel payloads, and share links are not private storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed to spin or edit the wheel. Sign-in is required for public share links, and paid plans are used for cloud library/ad-free workflows.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random team assignment only, one spin at a time. There is no guarantee of balanced group sizes, no constraints, no attribute balancing, no repeat minimization, and no quality diagnostics.

pros: Editable team names, colors, and weights
No account required for core spinning
Copy/download/share card for a spin result
Local wheel saving and general wheel import/export
cons: Does not generate full group rosters
No constraints, attributes, or repeat avoidance
No CSV/spreadsheet or Zoom group export

tools.name: RandomCount Team Generator

It eventually produces simple Team 1/Team 2/Team 3 results, but there is no paste-a-roster textarea, no copy/export, no group-count mode, no constraints, no saved roster, and no meaningful diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but weak random splitter. The one-at-a-time name entry alone makes it worse than most basic paste-and-generate tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding members one by one is tedious compared with pasting a class list. The rest is simple enough.
ratingComments.design: Plain Bootstrap-style utility page. Usable, but not polished.
ratingComments.features: Only add/remove members and members-per-team random splitting. No paste import, group-count mode, constraints, balancing, saved lists, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results display on the page only. No copy, CSV, PDF, share, or print-focused output.
ratingComments.privacy: The footer privacy policy is easy to find and it discloses Google Analytics/AdSense, location-derived information, generated-data language, and legal/service-provider sharing. The tool page also set Google Analytics cookies. Lighter than many ad-tech pages, but not ideal for roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even team size. Fine for trivial one-off use only.

pros: Not overloaded with a huge consent stack
cons: Vague privacy policy language around location and generated data

tools.name: Terrific Tools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: People who want a small browser tool with individual member entry and simple group-size controls

The problem is the workflow is clunkier than most simple splitters because it uses individual member fields instead of a normal paste-a-list textarea, and the page is wrapped in promotional banners, comments, account buttons, and a huge directory of unrelated random tools.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for generation; account prompts are for comments, ratings, bookmarks, and the broader site.
tools.overallComment: A weak random group generator with a few basic controls but worse input ergonomics than many simpler tools. The surrounding site noise and ad/privacy posture drag it down further.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The tool is usable, but individual member rows are a bad fit for a class or workshop roster. A simple textarea would be faster.
ratingComments.design: The actual form is readable, but the repeated launch banner, account/comment widgets, and enormous unrelated-tool grid make the page feel bloated.
ratingComments.features: Slightly above the weakest splitters: group title, members-per-group, dummy names, balance/remainder toggles, and dynamic members. No constraints, attributes, multi-round planning, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The page advertises copying groups to the clipboard, but there is no spreadsheet, CSV, image, PDF, Zoom, or share workflow visible in the basic form.
ratingComments.privacy: The site says tools process data in the browser, which is good for names. The privacy policy also discloses Google Analytics and Mediavine interest-based advertising, and the page sets CMP/ad-consent storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation itself is free and no-login. The account UI is mainly for ratings, comments, and bookmarks.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random grouping only. Balance/remainder toggles help group sizes, not assignment quality.

cons: Clunky member entry for real rosters
Google Analytics and Mediavine ad/privacy footprint

ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment. Fine for trivial splits, nothing more.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only.

tools.name: Sheffield Hallam TEL-e-vision Group Maker

tools.overallComment: A bare-bones random splitter. Useful in a pinch, but it is not competitive with modern group generators and has no real planning/export capability.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very simple: paste names, choose number of groups, click the button. The only friction is the dated page and confusing optional roles field.

tools.name: ClassTools Random Group Generator

ratingComments.resultQuality: Random only. This is what is advertised, but nothing more: no balancing, no constraints, no repeat reduction, and no quality diagnostics.

cons: Extremely weak despite high search-engine visibility
Search ranking far exceeds product quality

In live testing, participant names and team count could be entered, but the Generate button stayed disabled behind Cloudflare-style verification even after retry, so no actual teams, outputs, or constraint behavior could be verified. The page also loads PostHog/Plausible-style analytics and has broad privacy-policy language about personal data, cookies, tracking, marketing sharing, and account data.
tools.pricing: Public tool page with free-looking generator controls, upgrade/signup surfaces, and a disabled generate flow in live testing. No completed no-account generation was verified.
tools.overallComment: Feature claims are ambitious, but the reviewed generator did not actually generate teams in live testing because verification never completed.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The form is understandable, but live testing could not get past verification; Generate remained disabled after filling inputs and retrying verification.
ratingComments.design: Modern SaaS-style interface with clean fields and advanced settings, though the sidebar, upgrade prompts, verification, and generic AI-tool shell add friction.
ratingComments.features: Many controls are visible or claimed: distribution method, team names, captains, constraints, skill categories, custom requirements, language, quantity, and smarter AI. tools.overallComment: Excellent random picker, but barely a group generator. It only belongs here because people use it as a manual workaround for classroom grouping.

ratingComments.features: For group generation specifically, feature depth is very low. The public page did not expose a clear no-account form for pasting a roster, choosing group size/count, generating groups, copying results, or exporting. For actual group-generation work, this is much weaker than simple browser tools because it is prompt-based, opaque, and tied to the BasedLabs AI ecosystem.

tools.name: Teach Starter Random Name Picker
tools.bestFor: Picking one student at a time, not generating groups
tools.summary: Teach Starter has a free random name picker/spin-wheel classroom widget, but I could not find a real random group generator from Teach Starter. It can support manual grouping only by picking names one at a time or using saved word lists; it does not split a roster into teams.
tools.pricing: Free random name picker widget; broader Teach Starter resources and saved-account workflows may sit behind free/paid teacher plans, depending on region/resource.
tools.overallComment: Not a real group generator. Keep only if the article wants to mention classroom random-picking workarounds; otherwise it is a removal candidate.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For picking a single random name, it is easy. For generating groups, the workflow is bad because there is no group-generation workflow at all; you would have to pick names manually.
ratingComments.design: Polished and classroom-friendly compared with many utility sites. The issue is not presentation quality; it is that the relevant tool is a name picker, not a group maker.
ratingComments.features: Almost no group-generation feature depth. It can pick random names and use saved word lists, but it does not choose team count, group size, capacities, balancing, constraints, exports, or repeat avoidance.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful group output. At best it displays one selected name or spinner result. No team roster, copy/export surface, CSV, print groups, or shareable generated assignment.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than ad-tech-heavy utilities: no obvious AdSense mess on the widget page and Teach Starter has detailed education/privacy language including COPPA/PRIVO posture. A hosted education platform with accounts, cookies, server logs, location/IP/device data, marketing uses, analytics/customer tooling, and saved word-list/account behavior.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The random name picker is presented as free, but the broader Teach Starter ecosystem pushes account/resource workflows, and saved class lists depend on Teach Starter account features. No account is needed for the basic idea, but it is not as frictionless as a tiny standalone splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: No group result quality to credit. Random single-name selection may be fair, but it does not create balanced teams or group assignments.

pros: Free widget for simple random selection

cons: No team count or group size controls
No group roster output or export
Hosted platform collects normal account/log/cookie data
